title: SUFE (Slipped Upper Femoral Epiphysis)
oneLiner: A Salter-Harris type I fracture through the proximal femoral growth plate in adolescents, causing hip/groin pain and limp – a surgical urgency.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
You’re called to the paediatric assessment unit at 10pm. A 12-year-old boy, overweight, limps into the room. He’s been complaining of a dull ache in his right groin for three weeks, but his mother says he’s “just clumsy”. Tonight, after a minor fall during PE, he can’t put weight on that leg. He holds his right hip in external rotation and flexion. On exam, his right leg is shortened and externally rotated. When you try to internally rotate the hip, he screams – that’s his worst movement.

**Dissecting the vignette:**

- **Adolescent, overweight** → SUFE is a disease of the growing physis; obesity increases shear forces across the plate.
- **Dull groin ache for weeks** → prodromal “pre-slip” phase with synovitis and micro-slippage; often dismissed as “growing pains” or “muscle strain”.
- **Pain referred to groin** → hip pain is classically felt in the groin or referred to the medial thigh via the obturator nerve; if the child points to the lateral thigh or knee, think hip pathology (referred pain).
- **Acute-on-chronic after trivial injury** → the chronic slip becomes unstable; a minor twist forces the epiphysis to displace further.
- **Held in external rotation and flexion** → the femoral neck rotates anteriorly relative to the epiphysis; the leg assumes a position of comfort, which externally rotates the entire limb.
- **Short leg** → because the femoral head sits more posteromedially, the leg appears shortened.
- **Pain on internal rotation** → classic: internal rotation is limited and painful because it presses the femoral neck against the displaced epiphysis; external rotation is the path of least resistance.

**Atypical presentations that might trick you:**

1. **Knee-only pain** – The child points to the front of the knee (referred pain from the hip via the femoral nerve) and you waste time imaging the knee. Always examine the hip when a child presents with knee pain.
2. **Bilateral SUFE** – Up to 20–40% of cases are bilateral, especially in endocrine conditions (hypothyroidism, growth hormone therapy). The second side may be asymptomatic. Screening radiographs are mandatory.
3. **The non‑obese, tall, thin adolescent** – Think underlying endocrinopathy: renal osteodystrophy, hypoparathyroidism, or radiotherapy to the hip area. Don’t assume it’s only obese kids.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
SUFE is a fracture through the proximal femoral growth plate (physis) where the femoral head (epiphysis) slips posteriorly and inferiorly relative to the femoral neck. It is essentially a Salter-Harris type I injury that occurs in skeletally immature adolescents.

**Why it matters:**

- **Frequency** – It’s the most common hip disorder in adolescents (10–16 years), affecting about 1 in 1000 live births (higher in obese children).
- **Stakes** – A missed or delayed diagnosis leads to **avascular necrosis (AVN)** of the femoral head (the worst complication) – because the blood supply runs through the retinacular vessels along the posterior neck. If the slip is severe or untreated, these vessels kink.
- **Common pitfalls** – Over-reliance on the normal AP pelvic radiograph. The frog-leg lateral view is the diagnostic gold standard. Also, treating knee pain without imaging the hip.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The growth plate story:**

During adolescence, the proximal femoral physis is structurally weak due to rapid growth and hormonal changes. The physis is a cartilaginous disc between the metaphysis (neck) and epiphysis (head). Normally, the perichondrial ring and periosteum provide stability. In SUFE, the physis fails under shear stress.

**The slip sequence:**

1. **Pre‑slip (synovitis phase)** – The physis becomes thickened and weakened, often due to mechanical stress (obesity) or endocrine factors. The joint becomes irritable (synovitis). The child feels a dull ache but can walk.
2. **Acute slip** – The femoral head stays in the acetabulum, but the neck moves anteriorly and superiorly. The epiphysis appears to “slip” posteriorly and inferiorly relative to the neck. (Many find it easier to think: the neck moves forward, carrying the greater trochanter with it.)
3. **Stable vs unstable slip** – An **unstable** slip means the child cannot bear weight, even with crutches (the epiphysis is completely detached). A **stable** slip means they can bear weight (partial continuity). This distinction is critical for prognosis: unstable slips have a 50% AVN rate.

**Why the clinical signs happen:**

- **Pain with internal rotation** – The femoral neck rotates anteriorly, so internally rotation tries to force the neck into the acetabulum, impinging on the posteriorly displaced epiphysis. External rotation opens the joint space, feeling better.
- **Shortened, externally rotated leg** – The neck rides up, and the entire limb rotates outward because the piriformis muscle (which attaches to the greater trochanter) pulls the leg into external rotation when the hip is flexed.
- **Limp** – Pain and mechanical block (loss of internal rotation) cause an antalgic (painful) gait, often with the foot pointing outward.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Common Examples | Stem Clue |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common) | Adolescence + obesity + male sex | “Overweight 13-year-old boy with groin pain” |
| **Endocrine** | Hypothyroidism, growth hormone deficiency/surplus, hypogonadism, hypoparathyroidism | “Unusually tall or short for age, or delayed puberty” |
| **Metabolic** | Renal osteodystrophy (secondary hyperparathyroidism), rickets | “Chronic kidney disease, bowing legs” |
| **Mechanical** | High BMI, repetitive high-impact sports, previous hip irradiation | “Football player, overweight, or history of childhood cancer” |
| **Genetic** | Down syndrome, Marfan syndrome, Ehlers‑Danlos | “Known genetic syndrome, ligamentous laxity” |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic – but always consider endocrine in atypical body habitus or if bilateral.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** The diagnosis is radiological. There is no validated scoring system; instead, use **Klein’s line** on the AP pelvis and the **modified Barret’s angle** on the frog‑leg lateral.

- **Klein’s line** – On AP radiograph, draw a line along the superior border of the femoral neck. Normally it bisects the femoral head. In SUFE, it passes lateral to the head (the head has slipped below the line). This is the **single bedside radiograph clue**.
- **Frog-leg lateral view** – This is the **confirmatory view**. In SUFE, the femoral head lies posterior to the neck, and a line bisecting the neck (Barret’s line) will miss the head (modified angle > 10°).

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| AP Pelvis radiograph | Compare both hips; see Klein’s line, look for medial metaphyseal blanch sign (a double density) | First‑line screening; can be false‑negative in very early slips | Klein’s line fails to intersect the head = positive; treat as SUFE |
| Frog‑leg lateral | Especially sensitive for mild slips | Essential if AP is equivocal or normal but clinical suspicion high | Posterior displacement of head relative to neck; angle > 10° = slip |
| Ultrasound (hip) | Effusion can be seen; not diagnostic for SUFE, but can rule out septic arthritis | Child with fever and inability to weight‑bear – differentiate from septic hip | A sterile effusion does not exclude SUFE; if effusion + clinical SUFE pattern, get radiographs |
| MRI (hip) | Shows oedema in the physis and adjacent bone, and can detect pre‑slip | Rarely needed; reserved for unclear cases or to assess for AVN | High sensitivity for early slips; can show bone marrow oedema without displacement |
| Blood tests (FBC, CRP, ESR) | Rule out infection if septic arthritis is a differential | Fever, redness, raised inflammatory markers point away from SUFE | Normal/mildly raised CRP is common in SUFE; if markedly raised, think septic arthritis |

**Pattern recognition:**

- **Confirming image:** The frog‑leg lateral showing posterior slip is diagnostic.
- **Rule out:** A normal AP pelvis does not rule out SUFE. You must get a frog‑leg lateral.
- **Single finding that confirms:** Klein’s line failing to intersect the femoral head on AP (or, even better, posterior displacement on frog‑leg lateral).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – “Don’t miss, don’t reduce”**

- **What:** Obtain frog‑leg lateral radiographs. Place the child on strict non‑weight‑bearing with crutches. Do **not** attempt closed reduction of an acute slip – this increases AVN risk. Administer simple analgesia (paracetamol 15 mg/kg PO/IV, max 4 g/day; ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO, max 30 mg/kg/day if no contraindications).
- **Why:** An unstable slip is a surgical emergency. The femoral head’s blood supply is already tenuous; any movement can kink the retinacular vessels. The goal is to prevent progression before surgical fixation.
- **Call:** Orthopaedic consultant on call immediately. If unstable, the child should be kept supine with a pillow under the knee to maintain the leg in comfortable external rotation – do not force internal rotation.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – “Fix the slip”**

- **What:** Surgical in situ percutaneous pinning (or screw fixation). This is the standard of care. One or two cannulated screws are placed across the physis under image guidance to hold the epiphysis in position (the slip is NOT reduced; it is fixed in place). The child is kept nil‑by‑mouth preoperatively.
- **Why:** Pinning stabilises the physis, allows the growth plate to close (the screw induces physeal arrest), and prevents further slip. The goal is to preserve the remaining cartilage and blood flow – not to anatomically reduce the head.
- **Post‑op:** Usually weight‑bearing as tolerated after 6 weeks (stable slip) or non‑weight‑bearing for 6–12 weeks (unstable slip). Provide crutches, physiotherapy for gait training. DVT prophylaxis is rarely needed; encourage early mobilisation.

**Long‑term – “Screen the other side & watch for complications”**

- **What:** Obtain a single AP pelvis radiograph of the contralateral hip at diagnosis. If the physis is still open, some surgeons perform prophylactic pinning of the unaffected side if the child is high risk (obese, endocrine, very young). Otherwise, every 3‑6 months monitor for contralateral slip with radiographs until growth plate closure.
- **Why:** 20–40% of children develop bilateral SUFE, often within 18 months. Early detection of a contralateral slip prevents the same cascade.
- **Complication surveillance:** At 6 and 12 months, check for AVN (persistent pain, restricted movement, radiographic collapse) and femoroacetabular impingement (FAI) due to the residual deformity. If FAI develops, consider hip arthroscopy later.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**

- **Do not reduce the slip** – reduction of an acute unstable slip dramatically increases AVN rate (from ~5% stable to 50% unstable).
- **Do not prescribe NSAIDs without ensuring it’s not septic arthritis** – fever, painful passive range of motion, elevated CRP > 50 mg/L, or WBC > 12,000/mm³ should trigger hip aspiration.
- **Do not forget the contralateral hip** – always screen with an AP pelvis. If the other side is open, schedule follow‑up radiographs.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | SUFE | Septic Arthritis of Hip | Perthes Disease (Legg‑Calvé‑Perthes) | Transient Synovitis (Irritable Hip) |
|---------|------|------------------------|---------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| **Age** | 10–16 years (peak 12–14) | Any age, but peak <5 years | 4–8 years (peak 5–6) | 3–8 years |
| **Sex** | M > F (2:1), obese | Slight male predominance | M > F (4:1) | M > F (2:1) |
| **Pain** | Groin/medial thigh, dull, insidious then acute after trauma | Severe, acute, constant hip pain, intolerable to any movement | Hip/knee pain, intermittent, worse with activity (especially in boys) | Mild to moderate, worse in morning, resolves after several days |
| **Gait** | Antalgic, external rotation, limp | Non‑weight‑bearing, reluctant to move hip | Trendelenburg gait (lurch to affected side) | Antalgic, but often able to weight‑bear (walks with a limp) |
| **Examination** | Limited internal rotation, obligatory external rotation when flexing hip | Fever, hot swollen joint, severe pain with any passive range of motion (especially internal rotation) | Limited abduction and internal rotation, mild hip irritability | Mild discomfort at extremes of range, no fever, can crawl/weight‑bear |
| **Imaging** | AP pelvis + frog‑leg lateral: Klein’s line +, posterior slip | Ultrasound: effusion with thickened synovium; X‑ray: joint space widening, gas?; need aspiration | AP pelvis: femoral head fragmentation, collapse, coxa magna | US: simple effusion (no thickened synovium); X‑ray normal |
| **Labs** | CRP/ESR normal to mildly elevated; WBC normal | Marked elevated CRP/ESR, neutrophilia; blood culture positive in 50% | Normal ESR/CRP | Normal inflammatory markers |
| **Key distinguishing feature** | Frog‑leg lateral shows posterior slip; can bear weight (stable) or cannot (unstable), but pain on internal rotation is hallmark | Fever + inability to bear weight + raised CRP/ESR + aspiration shows bacteria | Age 4–8, classic radiographic changes of fragmentation, no acute slip | Self‑limiting, no fever, normal radiograph, resolves in days |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
1. **Avascular Necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head** – The most feared complication.
   - **Presentation:** Persistent pain, limping, progressive loss of hip motion (especially internal rotation). Radiographic collapse (crescent sign) appears 6–24 months after slip.
   - **Key signs:** Pain on weight‑bearing, Trendelenburg gait, leg length discrepancy (shortening). MRI shows absent bone marrow signal in the femoral head.
   - **What to do:** Urgent orthopaedic referral. Management may include core decompression, osteotomy, or total hip arthroplasty in late stages. AVN is irreversible – prevention (early pinning, avoid reduction) is paramount.

2. **Femoroacetabular Impingement (FAI)** – The residual bump formed by the displaced epiphysis acts as a cam lesion.
   - **Presentation:** Young adult with deep groin pain on sitting, climbing stairs, or after prolonged hip flexion. Limited internal rotation and positive impingement test (flexion + adduction + internal rotation).
   - **Key signs:** C‑sign (patient cups their hand over the lateral hip/groin). Radiographs show an aspherical femoral head, or a “pistol‑grip deformity”.
   - **What to do:** Hip arthroscopy to remove the cam lesion and repair labral tears. Early recognition prevents progression to osteoarthritis.

3. **Chondrolysis** – Loss of articular cartilage due to an inflammatory response or pin penetration.
   - **Presentation:** Severe stiffness, decreased range of motion (all planes), joint space narrowing on radiograph < 3 mm.
   - **What to do:** NSAIDs, physiotherapy, sometimes capsular release. Prevent by ensuring pins do not penetrate the subchondral bone.

4. **Progressive slip** – If untreated, the slip continues, leading to severe deformity and disability.
   - **Late presentation:** Adolescent who can no longer sit cross‑legged (because of external rotation contracture), severe leg length discrepancy (shortening), and gross limp. The femoral head may be completely posterior to the neck (severe slip >60°).
   - **What to do:** Realignment osteotomy (e.g., Southwick osteotomy) – high risk of AVN; often leads to early arthritis.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic: “SUFE slips – Screen All Kids Before You Miss It”**

- **S** – **S**creen the contralateral hip.
- **U** – **U**nstable? Avoid reduction – high AVN risk.
- **F** – **F**rog‑leg lateral is the gold standard.
- **E** – **E**ndocrine workup if bilateral, thin, or tall.

**Mnemonic: “Klein’s Line – if it misses the head, the slip is dead”** (meaning the diagnosis is confirmed).

**High‑yield clinical pearls (pattern‑recognition cues):**

1. **Any child with knee pain must have hip imaging** – the hip’s referred pain goes to the medial knee via the femoral nerve. A normal knee radiograph does not rule out SUFE.
2. **The frog‑leg lateral view is non‑negotiable** – up to 30% of SUFEs are missed on AP alone.
3. **A child who can bear weight is not necessarily safe** – stable slips can still progress. They still need pinning.
4. **If the child holds the leg in 60° of external rotation at rest, think septic arthritis** – SUFE usually has less severe external rotation.
5. **Bilateral SUFE in a thin child → check thyroid hormones** – hypothyroidism is a classic mimic.
6. **Do not manipulate an acute SUFE in the ED** – “look it but don’t move it”. Reduction risks AVN.
7. **After pinning, the child should have a single AP pelvis every 6 months until the contralateral physis closes** – don’t discharge them until then.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Missing the frog‑leg lateral – “the AP looks normal, so it’s not SUFE”.
- Trying to internally rotate the hip to see if it hurts – you already know it will. Just get the radiograph.
- Prescribing NSAIDs for “muscle strain” without imaging – you delay diagnosis and risk advanced slip.
- Forgetting to warn the family about AVN risk – informed consent is essential.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Situation | Who to Call | Threshold / Action |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Unstable slip** (child cannot bear weight) | Orthopaedic consultant on-call immediately | Arrange frog‑leg lateral, place child non‑weight‑bearing; do not reduce; prepare for urgent pinning |
| **Fever + hip pain + raised inflammatory markers** | Orthopaedics + consider septic arthritis | If CRP > 50 mg/L, ESR > 30, or WBC > 12,000/mm³, arrange hip aspiration. If purulent fluid, give empirical antibiotics (flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg IV q6h) |
| **Suspected bilateral SUFE** | Orthopaedics (discuss prophylactic pinning) | AP pelvis shows both slips; same‑day fixation |
| **Post‑op AVN suspicion** | Orthopaedics (urgent) | Persistent pain, restricted range, MRI shows avascular changes; refer to paediatric hip team |
| **Chondrolysis suspicion** | Orthopaedics (non‑urgent) | Joint space narrowing < 3 mm on radiograph; refer for management |
| **Symptoms of DVT or PE** (very rare but possible with prolonged non‑weight‑bearing) | Medical team / crash team | Sudden dyspnoea, chest pain, tachycardia; perform CTPA or V/Q; start LMWH per protocol |
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5 or GCS < 15** | Crash team / medical emergency team | Any postoperative child with tachycardia, hypotension, or altered consciousness – think sepsis, aspiration |

**Did you get all the key points?** If not, re‑read the pathophysiology section – that is where the conceptual understanding lives. Now go see that limping adolescent with a frog‑leg lateral and fix it before the head dies.

rawMarkdown:
## SUFE (Slipped Upper Femoral Epiphysis)

**One-liner:** A Salter-Harris type I fracture through the proximal femoral growth plate in adolescents, causing hip/groin pain and limp – a surgical urgency.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

You’re called to the paediatric assessment unit at 10pm. A 12-year-old boy, overweight, limps into the room. He’s been complaining of a dull ache in his right groin for three weeks, but his mother says he’s “just clumsy”. Tonight, after a minor fall during PE, he can’t put weight on that leg. He holds his right hip in external rotation and flexion. On exam, his right leg is shortened and externally rotated. When you try to internally rotate the hip, he screams – that’s his worst movement.

**Dissecting the vignette:**

- **Adolescent, overweight** → SUFE is a disease of the growing physis; obesity increases shear forces across the plate.
- **Dull groin ache for weeks** → prodromal “pre-slip” phase with synovitis and micro-slippage; often dismissed as “growing pains” or “muscle strain”.
- **Pain referred to groin** → hip pain is classically felt in the groin or referred to the medial thigh via the obturator nerve; if the child points to the lateral thigh or knee, think hip pathology (referred pain).
- **Acute-on-chronic after trivial injury** → the chronic slip becomes unstable; a minor twist forces the epiphysis to displace further.
- **Held in external rotation and flexion** → the femoral neck rotates anteriorly relative to the epiphysis; the leg assumes a position of comfort, which externally rotates the entire limb.
- **Short leg** → because the femoral head sits more posteromedially, the leg appears shortened.
- **Pain on internal rotation** → classic: internal rotation is limited and painful because it presses the femoral neck against the displaced epiphysis; external rotation is the path of least resistance.

**Atypical presentations that might trick you:**

1. **Knee-only pain** – The child points to the front of the knee (referred pain from the hip via the femoral nerve) and you waste time imaging the knee. Always examine the hip when a child presents with knee pain.
2. **Bilateral SUFE** – Up to 20–40% of cases are bilateral, especially in endocrine conditions (hypothyroidism, growth hormone therapy). The second side may be asymptomatic. Screening radiographs are mandatory.
3. **The non‑obese, tall, thin adolescent** – Think underlying endocrinopathy: renal osteodystrophy, hypoparathyroidism, or radiotherapy to the hip area. Don’t assume it’s only obese kids.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

SUFE is a fracture through the proximal femoral growth plate (physis) where the femoral head (epiphysis) slips posteriorly and inferiorly relative to the femoral neck. It is essentially a Salter-Harris type I injury that occurs in skeletally immature adolescents.

**Why it matters:**

- **Frequency** – It’s the most common hip disorder in adolescents (10–16 years), affecting about 1 in 1000 live births (higher in obese children).
- **Stakes** – A missed or delayed diagnosis leads to **avascular necrosis (AVN)** of the femoral head (the worst complication) – because the blood supply runs through the retinacular vessels along the posterior neck. If the slip is severe or untreated, these vessels kink.
- **Common pitfalls** – Over-reliance on the normal AP pelvic radiograph. The frog-leg lateral view is the diagnostic gold standard. Also, treating knee pain without imaging the hip.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The growth plate story:**

During adolescence, the proximal femoral physis is structurally weak due to rapid growth and hormonal changes. The physis is a cartilaginous disc between the metaphysis (neck) and epiphysis (head). Normally, the perichondrial ring and periosteum provide stability. In SUFE, the physis fails under shear stress.

**The slip sequence:**

1. **Pre‑slip (synovitis phase)** – The physis becomes thickened and weakened, often due to mechanical stress (obesity) or endocrine factors. The joint becomes irritable (synovitis). The child feels a dull ache but can walk.
2. **Acute slip** – The femoral head stays in the acetabulum, but the neck moves anteriorly and superiorly. The epiphysis appears to “slip” posteriorly and inferiorly relative to the neck. (Many find it easier to think: the neck moves forward, carrying the greater trochanter with it.)
3. **Stable vs unstable slip** – An **unstable** slip means the child cannot bear weight, even with crutches (the epiphysis is completely detached). A **stable** slip means they can bear weight (partial continuity). This distinction is critical for prognosis: unstable slips have a 50% AVN rate.

**Why the clinical signs happen:**

- **Pain with internal rotation** – The femoral neck rotates anteriorly, so internally rotation tries to force the neck into the acetabulum, impinging on the posteriorly displaced epiphysis. External rotation opens the joint space, feeling better.
- **Shortened, externally rotated leg** – The neck rides up, and the entire limb rotates outward because the piriformis muscle (which attaches to the greater trochanter) pulls the leg into external rotation when the hip is flexed.
- **Limp** – Pain and mechanical block (loss of internal rotation) cause an antalgic (painful) gait, often with the foot pointing outward.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Common Examples | Stem Clue |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common) | Adolescence + obesity + male sex | “Overweight 13-year-old boy with groin pain” |
| **Endocrine** | Hypothyroidism, growth hormone deficiency/surplus, hypogonadism, hypoparathyroidism | “Unusually tall or short for age, or delayed puberty” |
| **Metabolic** | Renal osteodystrophy (secondary hyperparathyroidism), rickets | “Chronic kidney disease, bowing legs” |
| **Mechanical** | High BMI, repetitive high-impact sports, previous hip irradiation | “Football player, overweight, or history of childhood cancer” |
| **Genetic** | Down syndrome, Marfan syndrome, Ehlers‑Danlos | “Known genetic syndrome, ligamentous laxity” |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic – but always consider endocrine in atypical body habitus or if bilateral.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** The diagnosis is radiological. There is no validated scoring system; instead, use **Klein’s line** on the AP pelvis and the **modified Barret’s angle** on the frog‑leg lateral.

- **Klein’s line** – On AP radiograph, draw a line along the superior border of the femoral neck. Normally it bisects the femoral head. In SUFE, it passes lateral to the head (the head has slipped below the line). This is the **single bedside radiograph clue**.
- **Frog-leg lateral view** – This is the **confirmatory view**. In SUFE, the femoral head lies posterior to the neck, and a line bisecting the neck (Barret’s line) will miss the head (modified angle > 10°).

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| AP Pelvis radiograph | Compare both hips; see Klein’s line, look for medial metaphyseal blanch sign (a double density) | First‑line screening; can be false‑negative in very early slips | Klein’s line fails to intersect the head = positive; treat as SUFE |
| Frog‑leg lateral | Especially sensitive for mild slips | Essential if AP is equivocal or normal but clinical suspicion high | Posterior displacement of head relative to neck; angle > 10° = slip |
| Ultrasound (hip) | Effusion can be seen; not diagnostic for SUFE, but can rule out septic arthritis | Child with fever and inability to weight‑bear – differentiate from septic hip | A sterile effusion does not exclude SUFE; if effusion + clinical SUFE pattern, get radiographs |
| MRI (hip) | Shows oedema in the physis and adjacent bone, and can detect pre‑slip | Rarely needed; reserved for unclear cases or to assess for AVN | High sensitivity for early slips; can show bone marrow oedema without displacement |
| Blood tests (FBC, CRP, ESR) | Rule out infection if septic arthritis is a differential | Fever, redness, raised inflammatory markers point away from SUFE | Normal/mildly raised CRP is common in SUFE; if markedly raised, think septic arthritis |

**Pattern recognition:**

- **Confirming image:** The frog‑leg lateral showing posterior slip is diagnostic.
- **Rule out:** A normal AP pelvis does not rule out SUFE. You must get a frog‑leg lateral.
- **Single finding that confirms:** Klein’s line failing to intersect the femoral head on AP (or, even better, posterior displacement on frog‑leg lateral).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – “Don’t miss, don’t reduce”**

- **What:** Obtain frog‑leg lateral radiographs. Place the child on strict non‑weight‑bearing with crutches. Do **not** attempt closed reduction of an acute slip – this increases AVN risk. Administer simple analgesia (paracetamol 15 mg/kg PO/IV, max 4 g/day; ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO, max 30 mg/kg/day if no contraindications).
- **Why:** An unstable slip is a surgical emergency. The femoral head’s blood supply is already tenuous; any movement can kink the retinacular vessels. The goal is to prevent progression before surgical fixation.
- **Call:** Orthopaedic consultant on call immediately. If unstable, the child should be kept supine with a pillow under the knee to maintain the leg in comfortable external rotation – do not force internal rotation.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – “Fix the slip”**

- **What:** Surgical in situ percutaneous pinning (or screw fixation). This is the standard of care. One or two cannulated screws are placed across the physis under image guidance to hold the epiphysis in position (the slip is NOT reduced; it is fixed in place). The child is kept nil‑by‑mouth preoperatively.
- **Why:** Pinning stabilises the physis, allows the growth plate to close (the screw induces physeal arrest), and prevents further slip. The goal is to preserve the remaining cartilage and blood flow – not to anatomically reduce the head.
- **Post‑op:** Usually weight‑bearing as tolerated after 6 weeks (stable slip) or non‑weight‑bearing for 6–12 weeks (unstable slip). Provide crutches, physiotherapy for gait training. DVT prophylaxis is rarely needed; encourage early mobilisation.

**Long‑term – “Screen the other side & watch for complications”**

- **What:** Obtain a single AP pelvis radiograph of the contralateral hip at diagnosis. If the physis is still open, some surgeons perform prophylactic pinning of the unaffected side if the child is high risk (obese, endocrine, very young). Otherwise, every 3‑6 months monitor for contralateral slip with radiographs until growth plate closure.
- **Why:** 20–40% of children develop bilateral SUFE, often within 18 months. Early detection of a contralateral slip prevents the same cascade.
- **Complication surveillance:** At 6 and 12 months, check for AVN (persistent pain, restricted movement, radiographic collapse) and femoroacetabular impingement (FAI) due to the residual deformity. If FAI develops, consider hip arthroscopy later.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**

- **Do not reduce the slip** – reduction of an acute unstable slip dramatically increases AVN rate (from ~5% stable to 50% unstable).
- **Do not prescribe NSAIDs without ensuring it’s not septic arthritis** – fever, painful passive range of motion, elevated CRP > 50 mg/L, or WBC > 12,000/mm³ should trigger hip aspiration.
- **Do not forget the contralateral hip** – always screen with an AP pelvis. If the other side is open, schedule follow‑up radiographs.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | SUFE | Septic Arthritis of Hip | Perthes Disease (Legg‑Calvé‑Perthes) | Transient Synovitis (Irritable Hip) |
|---------|------|------------------------|---------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| **Age** | 10–16 years (peak 12–14) | Any age, but peak <5 years | 4–8 years (peak 5–6) | 3–8 years |
| **Sex** | M > F (2:1), obese | Slight male predominance | M > F (4:1) | M > F (2:1) |
| **Pain** | Groin/medial thigh, dull, insidious then acute after trauma | Severe, acute, constant hip pain, intolerable to any movement | Hip/knee pain, intermittent, worse with activity (especially in boys) | Mild to moderate, worse in morning, resolves after several days |
| **Gait** | Antalgic, external rotation, limp | Non‑weight‑bearing, reluctant to move hip | Trendelenburg gait (lurch to affected side) | Antalgic, but often able to weight‑bear (walks with a limp) |
| **Examination** | Limited internal rotation, obligatory external rotation when flexing hip | Fever, hot swollen joint, severe pain with any passive range of motion (especially internal rotation) | Limited abduction and internal rotation, mild hip irritability | Mild discomfort at extremes of range, no fever, can crawl/weight‑bear |
| **Imaging** | AP pelvis + frog‑leg lateral: Klein’s line +, posterior slip | Ultrasound: effusion with thickened synovium; X‑ray: joint space widening, gas?; need aspiration | AP pelvis: femoral head fragmentation, collapse, coxa magna | US: simple effusion (no thickened synovium); X‑ray normal |
| **Labs** | CRP/ESR normal to mildly elevated; WBC normal | Marked elevated CRP/ESR, neutrophilia; blood culture positive in 50% | Normal ESR/CRP | Normal inflammatory markers |
| **Key distinguishing feature** | Frog‑leg lateral shows posterior slip; can bear weight (stable) or cannot (unstable), but pain on internal rotation is hallmark | Fever + inability to bear weight + raised CRP/ESR + aspiration shows bacteria | Age 4–8, classic radiographic changes of fragmentation, no acute slip | Self‑limiting, no fever, normal radiograph, resolves in days |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

1. **Avascular Necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head** – The most feared complication.
   - **Presentation:** Persistent pain, limping, progressive loss of hip motion (especially internal rotation). Radiographic collapse (crescent sign) appears 6–24 months after slip.
   - **Key signs:** Pain on weight‑bearing, Trendelenburg gait, leg length discrepancy (shortening). MRI shows absent bone marrow signal in the femoral head.
   - **What to do:** Urgent orthopaedic referral. Management may include core decompression, osteotomy, or total hip arthroplasty in late stages. AVN is irreversible – prevention (early pinning, avoid reduction) is paramount.

2. **Femoroacetabular Impingement (FAI)** – The residual bump formed by the displaced epiphysis acts as a cam lesion.
   - **Presentation:** Young adult with deep groin pain on sitting, climbing stairs, or after prolonged hip flexion. Limited internal rotation and positive impingement test (flexion + adduction + internal rotation).
   - **Key signs:** C‑sign (patient cups their hand over the lateral hip/groin). Radiographs show an aspherical femoral head, or a “pistol‑grip deformity”.
   - **What to do:** Hip arthroscopy to remove the cam lesion and repair labral tears. Early recognition prevents progression to osteoarthritis.

3. **Chondrolysis** – Loss of articular cartilage due to an inflammatory response or pin penetration.
   - **Presentation:** Severe stiffness, decreased range of motion (all planes), joint space narrowing on radiograph < 3 mm.
   - **What to do:** NSAIDs, physiotherapy, sometimes capsular release. Prevent by ensuring pins do not penetrate the subchondral bone.

4. **Progressive slip** – If untreated, the slip continues, leading to severe deformity and disability.
   - **Late presentation:** Adolescent who can no longer sit cross‑legged (because of external rotation contracture), severe leg length discrepancy (shortening), and gross limp. The femoral head may be completely posterior to the neck (severe slip >60°).
   - **What to do:** Realignment osteotomy (e.g., Southwick osteotomy) – high risk of AVN; often leads to early arthritis.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic: “SUFE slips – Screen All Kids Before You Miss It”**

- **S** – **S**creen the contralateral hip.
- **U** – **U**nstable? Avoid reduction – high AVN risk.
- **F** – **F**rog‑leg lateral is the gold standard.
- **E** – **E**ndocrine workup if bilateral, thin, or tall.

**Mnemonic: “Klein’s Line – if it misses the head, the slip is dead”** (meaning the diagnosis is confirmed).

**High‑yield clinical pearls (pattern‑recognition cues):**

1. **Any child with knee pain must have hip imaging** – the hip’s referred pain goes to the medial knee via the femoral nerve. A normal knee radiograph does not rule out SUFE.
2. **The frog‑leg lateral view is non‑negotiable** – up to 30% of SUFEs are missed on AP alone.
3. **A child who can bear weight is not necessarily safe** – stable slips can still progress. They still need pinning.
4. **If the child holds the leg in 60° of external rotation at rest, think septic arthritis** – SUFE usually has less severe external rotation.
5. **Bilateral SUFE in a thin child → check thyroid hormones** – hypothyroidism is a classic mimic.
6. **Do not manipulate an acute SUFE in the ED** – “look it but don’t move it”. Reduction risks AVN.
7. **After pinning, the child should have a single AP pelvis every 6 months until the contralateral physis closes** – don’t discharge them until then.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Missing the frog‑leg lateral – “the AP looks normal, so it’s not SUFE”.
- Trying to internally rotate the hip to see if it hurts – you already know it will. Just get the radiograph.
- Prescribing NSAIDs for “muscle strain” without imaging – you delay diagnosis and risk advanced slip.
- Forgetting to warn the family about AVN risk – informed consent is essential.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| Situation | Who to Call | Threshold / Action |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Unstable slip** (child cannot bear weight) | Orthopaedic consultant on-call immediately | Arrange frog‑leg lateral, place child non‑weight‑bearing; do not reduce; prepare for urgent pinning |
| **Fever + hip pain + raised inflammatory markers** | Orthopaedics + consider septic arthritis | If CRP > 50 mg/L, ESR > 30, or WBC > 12,000/mm³, arrange hip aspiration. If purulent fluid, give empirical antibiotics (flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg IV q6h) |
| **Suspected bilateral SUFE** | Orthopaedics (discuss prophylactic pinning) | AP pelvis shows both slips; same‑day fixation |
| **Post‑op AVN suspicion** | Orthopaedics (urgent) | Persistent pain, restricted range, MRI shows avascular changes; refer to paediatric hip team |
| **Chondrolysis suspicion** | Orthopaedics (non‑urgent) | Joint space narrowing < 3 mm on radiograph; refer for management |
| **Symptoms of DVT or PE** (very rare but possible with prolonged non‑weight‑bearing) | Medical team / crash team | Sudden dyspnoea, chest pain, tachycardia; perform CTPA or V/Q; start LMWH per protocol |
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5 or GCS < 15** | Crash team / medical emergency team | Any postoperative child with tachycardia, hypotension, or altered consciousness – think sepsis, aspiration |

**Did you get all the key points?** If not, re‑read the pathophysiology section – that is where the conceptual understanding lives. Now go see that limping adolescent with a frog‑leg lateral and fix it before the head dies.
